steps.title: Meet the Hyperbola
steps.goal: See how the shape of a hyperbola is defined.
steps.introText: A hyperbola has two branches. It is defined by the set of all points where the difference of the distances to two fixed points (the foci) stays constant. Note this is similar to the definition of an ellipse, but with a subtraction instead of an addition.
steps.prompt: Drag the point along a branch and observe that |d₁ − d₂| stays constant. Move the a and b handles to see how the shape of the hyperbola changes. Note: a is the **transverse semi-axis**, which is the distance from the center to the vertex, and b is the **conjugate semi-axis**, which controls the spread of the branches.

steps.title: Asymptotes and the Equation
steps.goal: See how the equation of a hyperbola is derived.
steps.introText: Centered at the origin, a hyperbola that opens left/right has the equation **x²/a² − y²/b² = 1**. The branches of the hyperbola follow two diagonal asymptotes with slope **±b/a** — derived as the diagonals of an a-by-b box.
steps.prompt: Drag the a and b handles to try at least three different shapes and see how the hyperbola changes. Pay close attention to how the box, the asymptotes, and the equation change.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Graph a hyperbola by identifying given its equation.

feedback.correct: Perfect! √9 = 3 and √4 = 2, so a = 3 and b = 2.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Remember that the denominators are a² and b², so take their square roots to find a and b.
feedback.hint: a² = 9 → a = 3 and b² = 4 → b = 2. Drag the a and b handles to match.

steps.title: Open It Upward
steps.goal: Flip the orientation with the toggle.
steps.info: When the y-term is positive and the x-term is negative, the hyperbola opens up and down instead of left and right. The equation then has the form **y²/a² − x²/b² = 1**. a is still called the transverse semi-axis, and b is still called the conjugate semi-axis.
steps.prompt: Use the orientation toggle to build y²/9 − x²/4 = 1.

feedback.correct: Yes! The positive y-term means it opens up and down, with a = 3 and b = 2.
feedback.incorrect: Use the toggle so the branches open up and down, then set a = 3 and b = 2.
feedback.hint: Tap “Opens up/down”, then make a = 3 (vertex) and b = 2.

steps.title: Find the Foci
steps.goal: Locate the foci of a hyperbola.
steps.info: The foci of a hyperbola are located at distance c from its center, with **c² = a² + b²**. They are placed in the same direction that the hyperbola opens. For instance, if the hyperbola opens left/right, the foci are located on the horizontal axis.
steps.prompt: A hyperbola centered at the origin and opening left/right has a = 3 and b = 4. Based on this information, where are its foci? You may use the graph below to help you visualize.
steps.feedback: Right: c² = 9 + 16 = 25, so c = 5 and the foci sit beyond the vertices at (±5, 0).
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Try setting a = 3 and b = 4 in the graph above and see where the foci land.

steps.title: Shift the Center
steps.goal: See how the equation of a hyperbola changes when the center is shifted.
steps.info: When the center of a (left/right opening) hyperbola is not necessarily at the origin, the equation generalizes to **(x − h)²/a² − (y − k)²/b² = 1**. For an up/down opening hyperbola, the equation generalizes to **(y − k)²/a² − (x − h)²/b² = 1**. Either way, (h, k) is the center of the hyperbola and a, b are the semi-axes. This should look very similar to the conic sections we've seen previously.

feedback.correct: Nice! The vertex is at (2, -1) while a = 3 and b = 2.
feedback.incorrect: Double check the center and your values of a, b. Make sure you are square rooting the denominators to get the values of a and b.
feedback.hint: Try and follow the outline provided in the graph. For further guidance, you can move the center to (2, −1) and set a = 3, b = 2.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Build four hyperbolas with the given orientation, size, and position — no hints.

hyperbolaSequence.label: Hyperbola that opens left/right centered at the origin (a = 4, b = 3)
Hyperbola that opens up/down centered at the origin (a = 3, b = 2)
Hyperbola that opens left/right, center (−2, 1), a = 2, b = 2

steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You now know the basics of hyperbolas. This concludes our brief introduction to conic sections.
